ABSTRACT
We report findings from a 40 middle school randomized controlled trial of an adapted version of 
Coping Power (Lochman & Wells, 2002a) for middle schoolers, called the Early Adolescent 
Coping Power (EACP) Program (Bradshaw et al., 2019) to determine the impact of EACP on 
adolescents ’ mental health outcomes, as indicated by student self-reported and teacher-ratings on 
the Behavior Assessment System for Children (BASC). The EACP was implemented over the 
course of students ’ 7th grade school year. The sample included 709 students who were identified 
at baseline through a teacher screening process for aggressive behavior and enrolled into the 
project (69.8 % African American and 59.4 % male). For teacher-reported outcomes, intent to 
treat (ITT) results indicated that EACP was associated with a decrease in externalizing problems 
over time. Exploration of effect modification suggested that girls in the EACP condition demon -
strated statistically significant decreases in school problems over time, as well as a baseline by 
intervention effect whereby students and schools with greater baseline difficulties demonstrated 
decreased internalizing problems over time. For student-reported outcomes, there was a signifi -
cant moderated effect, whereby girls in EACP demonstrated more favorable self-reported personal 
adjustment outcomes. Together, these results suggest that the early adolescent adaptation of 
Coping Power had preventive effects for 7th grade participants, and promotive effects specifically 
for girls, over time.
Aggressive and disruptive behavior problems often co-occur with poor academic functioning and low school connectedness 
( Herman et al., 2022 ; Reaves et al., 2018 ). These issues are particularly salient in middle school, where rates of school violence and 
disruption are higher relative to elementary school ( Monachino et al., 2021 ; NCES, 2022 ) and student engagement and caregiver 
involvement in school-based programming decline ( Wang & Eccles, 2012 ). Yet there are fewer school-based prevention programs to

address the behavioral and mental health concerns of middle school students, especially programs for students engaging in high rates 
of aggressive behaviors and demonstrating other indicators of risk for disengagement from school, academic problems, and longer- 
term mental health concerns ( Evans et al., 2007 ; Evans et al., 2017 ; Farmer et al., 2015 , also see Alperin et al., 2023 ; National 
Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine, 2019 ). Given the growing mental health crisis ( Office of the Surgeon General, 
2021 ; Weist et al., 2024 ), and schools’ access to youth, it is a valuable context for delivering interventions to meet their behavioral and 
mental health needs ( Evans et al., 2023 ; Santiago et al., 2018 ). Delivering preventive interventions in schools can increase access to 
care more generally, and address equity issues in the prevention of mental health problems among youth (see Alperin et al., 2023 ; 
Evans et al., 2007 ; Evans et al., 2023 ).
Coping Power ( Lochman & Wells, 2002a ) is a rigorously tested, school-based program that aims to prevent aggressive behavior 
problems in upper elementary students. This multi-component program includes clinician-facilitated group sessions for youth, 
separate group sessions for caregivers (i.e., parents), and select program activities for teachers. Though found to be effective with 
students in the upper elementary grades, prior to the present study, Coping Power had rarely been tested with youth in the middle 
school years, beyond 6th grade (for a review, see Lochman & Wells, 2002b ; Lochman et al., 2021 ; McDaniel et al., 2023 ). Given that 
previous studies have demonstrated a range of positive effects for Coping Power with upper elementary school children (i.e., grades 
4–6, within the elementary school setting), there was potential that these effects could also generalize to students in middle school. 
However, to increase the developmental fit of select program elements and activities, we made systematic developmental adaptations 
to the intervention, in turn creating a slightly adapted version of the model called the Early Adolescent Coping Power (EACP) Program 
( Bradshaw et al., 2017 ). Like the original, this adapted version of Coping Power was intended to meet the social, cognitive, and 
developmental needs of middle school students (grades 6–8) showing signs of difficulty self-regulating anger through supports to 
students, their caregivers, and teachers, to prevent further escalation of conduct and related behavior problems. Like earlier versions of 
Coping Power, EACP was designed to be delivered by school-based clinicians and counselors through schools during the school day, to 
overcome traditional barriers to services and inequities in access to services, including for students of color. EACP aims to prevent the 
escalation of these concerning behavior problems and avoid out of school placement for more intensive services ( Lochman & Wells, 
2002a ; National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, & Medicine, 2019 ).
The current study reports findings from a school-level randomized controlled efficacy study of EACP with students in 7th grade to 
determine impacts on teacher-rated externalizing problems at post-intervention and at a one-year follow-up. Secondary outcomes of 
interest included possible impacts on student self-reported concerns and the potential for cross-over outcomes to internalizing 
symptoms, as such effects have rarely been examined in prior studies. We also explored select effect modifiers beyond the intent to 
treat effects, including possible variation by race, sex, and baseline scores.
1. Background on coping power
Coping Power was designed to be an “indicated” prevention program ( National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, & Medicine, 
2019 ), to support students displaying aggressive behavior problems, with the goal of preventing later, and possibly worse, school and 
behavioral problems. Our focus in the current study on middle school-aged students is especially important, given the developmental 
literature highlighting the significance of this age group for concerns related to bullying, truancy, dropout, school failure, and tra -
jectories of aggressive behavior problems (e.g., Farmer et al., 2015 ; Farrell et al., 2018 ; Steinberg, 2016 ). Psychological functioning, 
self-esteem, and academic performance often tend to decline in the year following the middle school transition ( Eccles et al., 2018 ). 
Youth also display increases in substance use, delinquency, aggression, and other problem behaviors in middle school ( Farrell et al., 
2018 ; NCES, 2022 ; Steinberg, 2016 ).
Originally designed for children in upper elementary school (grades 4–5), Coping Power is based on a contextual social-cognitive 
developmental model ( Lochman & Wells, 2002a ). The multi-component program targets youth social cognitive and self-regulation 
processes which are hypothesized to mediate impacts on student outcomes. Similarly, a set of program activities occur for family/ 
caregivers (referred to in the current paper collectively as caregivers) which focus on practices and processes that aim to improve 
caregiver/child relationships and behaviors in the home environment, which extend into the community. A third set of activities target 
select teachers and focus on improving classroom-based supports. Specifically, these teachers engage in a series of activities to promote 
school bonding and academic organizational skills, with the goal of promoting academic success. For additional review of the Coping 
Power theoretical framework and the conceptualization of the mechanisms of change, see Lochman and Wells (2002a) and Lochman 
et al. (2008) . In the sections below, we describe the various components of the EACP version of Coping Power tested in the current 
study. We highlight some of the systematic developmental adaptations made to this version of Coping Power for youth in middle school 
(i.e., grades 6–8; see Bradshaw et al., 2017 ).
2. Overview of the EACP components tested in the current RCT
The Early Adolescent Coping Power (EACP; Bradshaw et al., 2017 ) version, like the original, is comprised of a student, caregiver, and 
teacher component and is based on a contextual social-cognitive developmental model ( Lochman & Wells, 2002a ). It was systemat -
ically adapted and feasibility tested through a NIMH funded grant to: (a) include fewer student sessions (i.e., reduced from 34 to 25), 
(b) be delivered over a single school year, and (c) incorporate content applicable to early adolescent students in grades 6 through 8. In 
the sections below, we describe the specific components and briefly summarize some of the adaptations made in creating the EACP 
version (for additional details on the adaptations see Bradshaw et al., 2017 ). For an overview of the original Coping Power youth 
curriculum and the theorized mechanisms of change see Lochman et al. (2008) .
C.P. Bradshaw et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                  Journal of School Psychology 110 (2025) 101437 
2

The EACP Youth Component as delivered in the context of this randomized controlled trial (RCT) included both a group and 
individual component. The youth group component included 6–7 students and took place at the students’ schools, typically during 
nonacademic homeroom periods, recess, specials, or lunch. The 25 group sessions lasted approximately 45 min and were designed to 
fit within a single course period. Group youth sessions delivered in this RCT were held approximately once week over the school year 
(spanning nearly 9 months). The group sessions in this RCT were co-led by a grant-funded intervention staff member (i.e., master’s, 
doctorate-level clinician, or clinician trainee) and when possible, were co-facilitated by the schools’ counselor, social worker, or school 
psychologist. Together, the group sessions were designed to foster students’ social-cognitive development ( Lochman et al., 2008 ).
The first eight sessions (Phase 1) focused on the delivery of content and concepts to the students and on developing group norms, 
setting short- and long-term goals, organizational and study skills, awareness of feelings, anger coping and self-control, coping self- 
statements, relaxation techniques, and perspective-taking. In the 9th through 15th session (Phase 2), students learned to apply the 
concepts taught by practicing perspective-taking and social problem-solving using the “PICC” (Problem Identification Choices Con -
sequences) model. This phase culminated in the student group planning and creating a video to teach others the concepts that they had 
learned. In the final phase (i.e., sessions 16–25), developmental adaptations were made to support the students in applying their skills 
to scenarios and challenges early adolescents face, including friendship and romantic relationship development, social aggression, 
cyberbullying, problem-solving about damaged relationships and neighborhood problems, refusal skills/coping with peer pressure, 
deviant peer groups and centrality of group membership, positive quality development, entering new peer groups, and using positive 
peer networks. Students were also taught to utilize assertive “I-statements.”
In addition, EACP was designed so that each child also received a total of 8–10 individual 30-min sessions with the group leaders 
during school hours to further build rapport, monitor progress, and reinforce content (see Lochman et al., 2008 ). The individual 
sessions were led by the clinician to encourage generalization of the skills developed during the group sessions (see Lochman et al., 
2008 ). The individual sessions were used primarily for monitoring and reinforcing attainment of classroom and social behavior goals 
(e.g., avoiding fights, resisting peer pressure) and coping with specific attributional biases and social problem-solving challenges.
The EACP Caregiver Component consisted of 12 group sessions for caregivers, implemented over the same school year as the 
student component. These sessions focused on building caregiver skills in behavior management, supporing the new skills students 
were learning in student groups, and to promote a more positive school experience for students. Caregivers met in groups of 10–12 with 
the two co‑leaders of the youth component. The EACP caregiver component was derived from social learning theory-based caregiver 
training programs developed and evaluated by prominent clinician-researchers in the field of child aggression ( Lochman et al., 2008 ). 
Over the course of the 12 sessions, caregivers received training on a variety of skills including rewarding wanted behaviors, giving 
effective instructions, establishing age-appropriate rules and expectations for adolescents, applying effective consequences to un -
wanted behavior, establishing ongoing family communication structures (e.g., weekly family meetings), and effective monitoring. 
Efforts were made to include all caregivers in groups; we incorporated multiple engagement activities, such as motivational inter -
viewing strategies ( Herman et al., 2012 ), to increase engagement and caregiver attendance. Additional structural modifications and 
activities were provided to increase attendance, such as offering caregiver sessions at flexible times (e.g., after school and in the 
evening). We also offered transportation / taxi vouchers, child friendly activities for siblings during the sessions, and food to increase 
parental attendance.
In addition to these “standard” caregiver skills, caregivers also learned to support the social-cognitive and problem-solving skills 
that youth learned in the EACP youth component. Caregiver skills were introduced at the same time as the respective youth skills, so 
that caregivers and youth could work together at home to reinforce what they were learning in the program. For example, caregivers 
learned to set up homework support structures and reinforce organizational skills. Caregivers learned to support students’ bonding to 
school and methods for positive involvement in the school setting (e.g., setting up productive, proactive meetings with teachers, and 
maintaining contact). Caregivers also learned to apply the social problem-solving model to the family so that the students’ skills 
learned in the group would be prompted and reinforced at home. The sessions also addressed parenting adolescents, communication, 
and some issues related to bullying and peer relationships among adolescents.
Finally, the EACP Teacher Component targeted identified “cheer teachers” for the Coping Power intervention to support their 
generalization of skills and to monitor progress. A teacher was identified for each participating student (i.e., in some cases, one teacher 
served as a “cheer teacher” for more than one student; on average teachers served as the cheer teacher for 1–4 students, with a modal 
number of 3 students per teacher). The teachers were selected if they were core content teachers (e.g., language arts, mathematics) or 
homeroom teachers, to ensure they would have sufficient access to the participating students. These teachers engaged in three 30-min 
meetings during the school year with an EACP clinician. These meetings included a didactic presentation on EACP, reviewing its 
theoretical approach, the structure of student sessions, and identification of the key skills students were learning so that teachers could 
support and optimize their skill use in the classroom. Teachers received instruction on how to support students in setting goals, 
reviewing weekly tip sheets of topics and activities, discussing goal progress and challenges, encouraging students to apply skills 
developed in the program, building positive relationship with students, and communicating with the school counselor and EACP 
clinician about student concerns and progress. Teachers received written instructions on how to implement these activities; the 
implementation of the EACP teacher content was manualized. Cheer teachers were expected to deliver EACP teacher content during 
their routine interactions in the classroom to help promote generalization in the classroom setting and within the peer group.
3. Prior research on coping power
Although this is the first efficacy study of EACP, several RCTs of the original version of Coping Power have demonstrated significant 
improvements in younger children’s disruptive behavior problems, both in the short and long term (e.g., 4-year follow-up) (e.g., 
C.P. Bradshaw et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                  Journal of School Psychology 110 (2025) 101437 
3

Lochman et al., 2013 ; Lochman & Wells, 2002b, 2003, 2004 ). These earlier studies with younger children have also shown preventive 
effects on associated outcomes, such as reduced substance use ( Lochman & Wells, 2003, 2004 ), and improved grades ( Lochman et al., 
2012 ). Specifically, Coping Power reduced aggression at the end of the intervention period and at 1-, 3-, and 4-year follow-ups, and 
improved school behavior outcomes (e.g., reductions in disciplinary referrals, suspensions) and student concentration, on-task 
behavior, and study skills ( Lochman et al., 2013 ; Lochman & Wells, 2002b, 2003, 2004 ). The efficacy studies of the full-length, 
multicomponent Coping Power program in comparison to school-as-usual comparison groups at one-year follow-ups after interven -
tion ( Lochman & Wells, 2003, 2004 ) found small effect sizes for intervention effects on covert delinquency (effect sizes [ES] =
0.28 – 0.36) and on teacher-rated behavior problems (ES = 0.34 – 0.35), and medium effect sizes for substance use (ES = 0.58 – 0.64). 
Placing these findings in context, school-based prevention studies that have effectively reduced crime, substance use and conduct 
problems typically have small effect sizes ( Wilson et al., 2001 ).
A selective meta-analysis by McDaniel et al. (2023) examining a set of 12 pre-post studies of adapted versions of Coping Power 
across a broad range of outcomes (internalizing and information-processing, in addition to the primary externalizing outcomes) and 
applications (including when delivered as a universal intervention, as a briefer intervention, as a dissemination study, or within a 
specialized school for deaf children) of Coping Power demonstrated “ small to moderate reductions in externalizing behavior, although 
these findings were not statistically significant ” ( McDaniel et al., 2023 , p. 350); specifically, they noted an “ overall mean effect size of g 
= � .15 ( p = 0.252; CI[-0.42, 0.12] ” ( McDaniel et al., 2023 , p. 365) based on 55 effect sizes based across 12 studies. The lack of 
statistical significance despite meaningful effect sizes may be due to limited power for statistical testing in meta-analyses with rela -
tively few studies ( Pigott & Polanin, 2015 ).
Prior pilot research on EACP. With funding through a NIMH grant, the EACP was feasibility and acceptability tested, and pilot 
tested in a subsequent under-powered study with 30 middle school students ( Bradshaw et al., 2017 ). Pre-post-intervention results on 
the teacher-report version of the Behavioral Assessment for Children- 2nd Edition (BASC-2) indicated that the program was associated 
with reductions in externalizing problems and hyperactivity, and improvements in adaptive skills and leadership ( Bradshaw et al., 
2017 ). Together, the findings from previous RCTs of the original Coping Power program and the pilot work on EACP provided the 
rationale for testing the EACP with middle schoolers in a more rigorous study examining a broader set of outcomes.
4. Overview of the current study
The current study presents findings from a school-level RCT of EACP as implemented in seventh grade. We report findings con -
trasting baseline of 7th grade with the end of 7th grade, and at one-year follow-up (end of 8th grade) on students ’ behavior at school. 
We assessed outcomes based on student self-report and teacher report to determine the impact of EACP on social, emotional, and 
behavior problems. Most Coping Power research to date has focused on teacher ratings of student behavioral outcomes, but little 
research has included student self-reported behaviors, likely due to the historical focus on younger student participants in the Coping 
Power program. Our primary aim focused on intent to treat analyses to determine the main effects of the EACP preventive intervention 
on key teacher-reported externalizing outcomes of interest that put youth at risk for a range of behavioral and mental health problems. 
Consistent with prior work by Lochman and Wells (2002b, 2003, 2004) (also see Lochman et al., 2013 ; McDaniel et al., 2023 ), we 
anticipated that EACP would have a significant impact on teacher ratings of externalizing problems, which was our primary outcome of 
interest.
We were also interested in exploring for the potential of youth self-reported outcomes of externalizing problems, and the possibility 
of cross-over effects on internalizing symptoms ( Clifford et al., 2020 ). The inclusion of youth self-report data and internalizing 
problems are an important and unique contribution of the current study. Specifically, this assessment of internalizing symptoms 
allowed for the exploration for transdiagnostic effects of EACP on depression and anxiety, as there is some research suggesting that 
Coping Power may have an impact on these factors in addition to the typical impacts on externalizing outcomes ( Clifford et al., 2020 ). 
Documenting such impacts may suggest a broader set of intervention effects on mental health outcomes for EACP than previously 
assessed, which is especially timely given the rising rates of mental health concerns among youth ( Office of the Surgeon General (OSG), 
2021 ; Weist et al., 2024 ). Given these effects were exploratory and secondary, we did not have specific hypotheses about these out -
comes. Finally, we explored how the impact of EACP may have been moderated by sex, race, and baseline levels of problem behaviors.
5. Method
5.1. Study design
We conducted a school-level group RCT ( Murray, 1998 ) to evaluate the effects of EACP on 7th grade students ’ behavior in 40 
middle schools in Alabama and Maryland, with 20 schools enrolled in each state. The total sample of 40 schools was recruited into the 
project through district partnerships with the researchers. Half of the schools in each state were randomized to implement the 
intervention and half were assigned to school as usual, just once, prior to the screening of the first year of the project. Schools were 
randomized to either a control or intervention condition to avoid contamination effects that could occur if students were randomized 
within schools. We created 20 matched pairs of schools and randomly assigned schools within pairs to EACP and control. Participating 
schools had a mean enrollment of 569.03 students ( SD = 238.11) and racially diverse student populations (e.g., M = 58.02 % African 
American, SD = 34.44). In addition, schools reported, on average, that 68.06 % ( SD = 26.33) of students in their school were eligible 
for free or reduced-price meals.
Three consecutive cohorts of approximately 120 students each year were included in the intervention condition and contrasted to

the 120 control students each year. Data were collected from intervention and control students at three time points over the course of 
two years (one intervention year and one follow-up year). The students identified for inclusion in the study were selected from the 
approximately 25 % of rising seventh graders in the sample who evidenced the highest levels of aggression relative to their peers, 
according to teacher ratings (see details on screening procedure below). Identical screening procedures were used in both conditions; 
however, in the intervention schools, those youth who were screened and consented to participate entered an EACP intervention group 
(comprised of 6 – 7 students). One group was formed in each intervention school and the intervention was provided to the students over 
the course of a school year. This same process was conducted with a new cohort of 7th grade students in cohorts 2 and 3. The data for 
this project were collected between fall of 2014 and spring of 2018 (follow up).

5.2. Participants
The sample included 709 7th graders. In the 20 schools randomized to implement the program, 10 in Alabama and 10 in Maryland, 
377 youth were allocated to receive the intervention whereas 332 students were allocated to participate in school-as-usual (see 
CONSORT diagram in Fig. 1 ). The final sample of participating students was 69.82 % African American and 59.38 % male. At baseline, 
over 50 % of the sample was in the at-risk or clinically significant range for low adaptive skills and high externalizing problems as 
measured on the Behavioral Assessment for Children, 2nd Edition (BASC-2 Adolescent, ages 12 – 21; Reynolds & Kamphaus, 2004 ); 
approximately 20 % was at-risk for or clinically significant for internalizing problems on the BASC-2. Additional demographic in -
formation, including baseline levels of emotional and behavioral difficulties, is reported in Tables 1 and 2 .
5.3. Procedures
5.3.1. Screening
During the previous spring academic semester, sixth grade teachers completed a universal, six-item screening measure (Reactive 
and Proactive Aggression Questionnaire [ Dodge et al., 1997 ]) assessing rising 7th grade student proactive and reactive aggression 
( Lochman & Conduct Problems Prevention Research Group, 1995 ). Students who received a score of 11 on a possible range of 6 – 30 on 
proactive and reactive aggression (i.e., tending to be ranked in the highest quarter [25 %] of students) were recruited to begin 
participation in the study during the following year (i.e., the fall of seventh grade) and comprised the eligible study sample. Partic -
ipation was voluntary. A list of eligible students was generated based on their screening data and parents were contacted by project 
staff using the contact information provided by the school. Children were enrolled on a first come first serve basis until the seven EACP 
group positions were filled per school, first obtaining the parental consent for their child ’ s participation and for their own (parental) 
participation, and then the youth assent. After the participants were enrolled in the program, the baseline data were collected from the 
teachers, youth, and parents (as described below). All study data were collected in fall of 7th grade at baseline prior to beginning EACP 
intervention groups, at the end of the 7th grade school year (post-test), and in the spring of 8th grade as the one-year follow-up. All 
measures are described below. The study was approved by the institutions ’ Institutional Review Boards.
5.3.2. EACP implementation fidelity data
The intervention groups were conducted during the students ’ 7th grade year. On average, students attended 78.82 % of sessions (i. 
e., a mean of 18.58 out of the 25 sessions; valid range 0 to 25; SD = 6.07). Student attendance data showed that 57.29 % of the students 
attended 80 % or more of the sessions and 81.7 % of the students attended 60 % or more of the sessions. Approximately 61.7 % of the 
groups received all 25 sessions; an additional 16.7 % of the groups had 24 sessions delivered; 8.3 % had 23 sessions delivered, 3.3 % 
had 22 sessions delivered; and 10 % had 21 sessions delivered in this study. Students also received an average of 3.10 individual 
contacts with the clinician ( SD = 2.22, range = 0 – 15), and spent an average of 65.65 total minutes with the group leader in these 
contacts ( SD = 70.75, range = 0 – 510 min), indicating high adherence to the individual component of EACP. To assess youth 
participant engagement, group leaders answered 8 items from the Groupwork Engagement Measure ( Macgowan, 1997 ) regarding each 
student in the group. This measure has been used in prior counseling research (e.g., Prado et al., 2006 ), with higher scores on this four-

There was high clinician adherence to the program, whereby clinicians implemented 24.13 (out of the possible 25) sessions on 
average and implemented 71.81 % of all objectives for all 25 sessions. It is worth noting there are extra activities and objectives in each 
session to allow for some clinician discretion. For additional details on EACP implementation, see Pas et al. (2020) (also see a prior 
developmental study by Bradshaw et al., 2017 regarding program feasibility and acceptability). Caregivers ’ attendance ranged from 
zero to all 12 full sessions, with 40.11 % of caregivers attending one or more sessions. On average, caregivers attended 2.08 of the 12 
sessions.
5.4. Equivalence at baseline
Given the school randomized design, we examined baseline equivalence of intervention and control schools at the school level. 
Specifically, we examined between-group baseline differences on school demographic characteristics, school-level student-reported 
BASC-2 composites, and teacher-reported BASC-2 composites (see means, standard deviations, and results of the t -tests in Table 2 ). 
Results of the school level independent samples t -tests suggested that there were no statistically significant differences on school 
demographics, or student-reported BASC-2 composites. There were, however, significant baseline differences on two of the teacher- 
reported BASC-2 scores (i.e., Externalizing Problems composite, t (38) = � 2.20, p = .034, d = 0.69, and Internalizing Problems 
composite, t (38) = � 2.03, p = .049, d = 0.64), such that the intervention group schools demonstrated significantly more difficulties at 
baseline. However, the independent t -tests for the teacher-reported Adaptive Skills, t (38) = 1.86, p = .071, and School Problems, t (38) 
= � 1.83, p = .075, were not statistically significant at the p < .05 level.
5.5. Measures
5.5.1. Student behavior
Teachers and students reported on students ’ emotional and behavioral health using the Behavioral Assessment System for Children 
(BASC-2: Adolescent, ages 12 – 21; Reynolds & Kamphaus, 2004 ). The BASC-2 has extensive research documenting reliability and 
validity evidence for the youth and teacher reports ( Reynolds & Kamphaus, 2004 ). Both the student- and teacher-reported scales were 
collected at baseline, post-intervention, and at one-year follow-up. Teachers and students rated the frequency of each behavior on a 
four-point Likert scale ranging from never (0) to always (3).

5.6. Analytic plan
A series of bivariate tests of predictors of missingness/loss-to-follow-up were conducted for all four student-reported and four 
teacher-reported outcomes at post- and follow-up. For teacher-reported outcomes, intervention assignment, and site were significantly 
associated with missingness at post-assessment, while site, baseline BASC-2 scores, and student race were significantly associated with 
missingness at the one-year follow-up. For student-reported outcomes, intervention assignment, student race, and cohort were 
associated with missingness at post-assessment, while site and baseline BASC-2 scores were associated with missingness at follow-up. 
Covariates found to be associated with missingness at post- or follow-up were included in the model to account for data that were not 
missing completely at random.
We conducted a series of three-level, multilevel models ( Heck & Thomas, 2015 ) in M plus ( Muth ´en & Muth ´en, 1998 – 2017 ) to 
examine the impact of the intervention on growth in outcomes from baseline to one-year follow-up. A separate model was fit for each 
BASC-2 outcome (i.e., four teacher-reported composites and four student-reported composites). Repeated measures were accounted for 
at level one while student- and school-level associations were modeled at level two and level three, respectively. Full-information 
robust maximum likelihood was utilized to account for multivariate non-normality in the data as well as missing data that were 
assumed to be missing at random ( Enders & Bandalos, 2001 ; Muth ´en & Asparouhov, 2002 ). Covariates were the same in all final 
teacher- and student reported outcome models. At level one (repeated measures), we estimated two random effects as growth over

To examine baseline by intervention interactions, we reparametrized the model as a latent growth model under the specifications of

between-school levels. In the EACP group, in addition to the initial status and growth factors, an additional growth factor was esti -
mated which represents the additional growth over time that is attributable to EACP. The covariances between the initial status factor 
and additional slope parameter in EACP schools at the within- and between-levels represents the baseline by intervention interaction. 
The significance of these terms was determined by the respective Wald tests ( p < .05). All covariates were included as indicated above.

6.2. Sensitivity analyses: inverse probability of intervention weighting
We also conducted a series of sensitivity analyses related to baseline imbalance across EACP and school-as-usual schools (see 
Supplementary Tables). Specifically, the sensitivity analyses added inverse probability of intervention weights (IPTW) to the primary 
intent-to-treat models. Model-specific, school level weights were created by estimating the probability of intervention assignment 
conditional on baseline values of the focal BASC-2 outcome. The inverse was utilized as a school-level weight in the three-level ITT 
models. Covariates were included in a penultimate model, as described above but retained only if significantly related to growth. 
Taken together, the results of the sensitivity analyses were largely consistent with the results presented above, particularly with regard 
to the ITT point estimates. These in turn give us more confidence in the overall pattern of findings, despite the baseline imbalance on 
the two teacher-reported BASC-2 scores.
7. Discussion
This study aimed to contribute to the knowledge base on Coping Power, as there has been limited examination of the impacts of this 
and other prevention programs during the middle school years ( Alperin et al., 2023 ; Evans et al., 2007 ). These results suggest that the 
structured social-cognitive indicated prevention program, which includes youth, caregiver, and teacher elements, can be adapted for 
use with early adolescents, and have a significant main effect on teacher ratings of externalizing problems through a 1-year follow up. 
Notably, 8th grade teachers, who completed the follow-up assessment were unaware of the intervention condition. There was also an 
interaction between sex and intervention on School Problems over time, such that girls in EACP demonstrated decreases in School 
Problems over time.
The intervention effect on teacher-reported adolescent externalizing behavior is notable because of the relative dearth of indicated 
school-based, evidence-based interventions for youth with difficulties regulating aggression during the middle school years, especially 
in relation to the growing intervention literature for similar children in the preadolescent years ( Alperin et al., 2023 ; Daly et al., 2018 ; 
Evans et al., 2007 ). Although a number of rigorous studies have demonstrated that classroom-level universal prevention in the middle 
school years can have significant positive effects on youth substance use, antisocial behavior, aggression and bullying (e.g., Botvin 
et al., 2006 ; Espelage et al., 2015 ; Marshall et al., 2024 ) the effects of multicomponent interventions targeting early adolescents with 
aggressive behavior problems has been limited and sometimes discouraging. For example, the comprehensive multicomponent Fast 
Track program that provided school-based intervention to children with behavior problems and their families in a sustained way, from 
first grade through tenth grade, had its significant positive effects evident during elementary school ( Conduct Problems Prevention 
Research Group, 2002, 2004 ) fade dramatically during the middle school years ( Conduct Problems Prevention Research Group, 2010 ), 
before re-emerging again in later adolescence and young adulthood ( Conduct Problems Prevention Research Group, 2015 ). The dif -
ficulty in obtaining stable positive effects in targeted interventions with at-risk adolescents in middle school may be due to the dra -
matic changes that occur during adolescence in youths ’ hormonal and self-regulatory processes, and in their peer relations which can 
be increasingly influenced by deviant peer associations ( Utrzan et al., 2018 ). These changes in self-regulatory and peer processes can 
negatively affect youth response to small group interventions (e.g., Dishion & Andrews, 1995 ) and to their behavioral development. In 
this context, then, it is encouraging that EACP can help reduce the escalation of externalizing behaviors among at-risk students during 
this developmental period; moreover, these effects are apparent for boys and girls, and for Black and White students. This develop -
mentally adapted version of Coping Power likely reduces the students ’ externalizing problems by addressing the developmentally 
salient emotional regulation, decision-making, and peer relationship skills and the caretakers ’ monitoring and consistent discipline 
skills.
The student-self-reported outcomes were exploratory and secondary to the teacher-reported outcomes because this was among the 
first study of Coping Power to explore self-reported outcomes. In fact, youth self-reports of externalizing behavior have not been found 
to be as related to occurrence of externalizing disorders as are teacher and parent/caregiver reports ( Salbach-Andrae et al., 2009 ), and 
thus child behavioral reports may not be as predictive to their later serious behavioral outcomes as are teacher reports. Moreover, 
teacher reports may be more reliable sources of information on students ’ behavior in the school setting, whereas students may lack 
C.P. Bradshaw et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                  Journal of School Psychology 110 (2025) 101437 
10

insight on the impact of their behavior in relation to others, especially in the school context. Furthermore, teachers are natural raters 
and their perspectives of students’ behavior play an important role in the education context (for further discussion of discrepancies in 
reporters, see De Los Reyes et al., 2023 ). The results suggested that EACP was associated with a small, statistically significant increase 
in Internalizing Problems over time, although not leading to overall mean scores in the clinical range. Some children with externalizing 
problems have low levels of subjective anxiety, are less concerned about consequences of their behavior and thus have poorer out -
comes ( Fanti et al., 2016 ; Frick & Wall Myers, 2018 ). Modest levels of anxiety and depression have been associated with better 
intervention response among children with disruptive behaviors ( Jarrett et al., 2014 ; Kazdin & Whitley, 2006 ; Ollendick et al., 2008 ), 
and, in the present study, modest increases in Internalizing Problems may indicate children’s increased awareness of consequences of 
externalizing behaviors. More research to replicate this finding, including measures on potential mechanisms (e.g., increased insight 
and awareness), would benefit the field.
In addition to EACP effects on the primary outcome of this intervention, namely externalizing behavior problems, EACP also 
reduced the escalation of teacher-reported internalizing problems among adolescents with high baseline levels of internalizing 
problems, and among those attending schools with at-risk peers who also had high rates of internalizing behaviors. Comorbidity of 
externalizing and internalizing symptoms can be evident among a subgroup of aggressive youth. Students who have an angry-irritable 
subtype of oppositional behaviors can have comorbid depression symptoms both concurrently and longitudinally, likely due to un -
derlying emotion regulation difficulties, and be at risk for future suicidal behaviors ( Evans et al., 2017 ). Those with both conduct 
problems and depressive symptomatology are also more likely to engage in substance use as adolescents than those children with 
conduct problems alone ( Miller-Johnson et al., 1998 ). While EACP may be considered a disorder-specific intervention given its focus 
on children with aggressive behavior issues, it may contain certain transdiagnostic elements that impact other presenting symptoms 
along with externalizing behaviors ( Clifford et al., 2020 ). In fact, similar to outcomes from other interventions for preadolescents (e.g., 
Chase & Eyberg, 2008 ; Kazdin, 2010 ), research has shown that Coping Power delivered in the elementary school years can produce 
reductions in both externalizing and internalizing behaviors ( Lochman et al., 2015 ), and has been particularly effective in reducing 
externalizing behaviors for students who have higher baseline depression ( Jarrett et al., 2014 ). The current findings extend these 
transdiagnostic intervention effects to Coping Power as adapted and implemented during the middle school years.
EACP may have these effects either because (a) some internalizing problems follow youth’s development of aggressive behaviors 
and therefore changes in aggression may influence these outcomes, or (b) there are some common active mechanisms within the 
program that may affect multiple areas ( Clifford et al., 2020 ; Lochman et al., 2019 ). EACP appears to target a number of common risk 
factors that predict both externalizing and internalizing problems, including psychosocial stressors in varied environmental contexts 
(school, neighborhood, peer group), difficult temperament (e.g., irritability, restlessness, lack of persistence, low adaptability), social- 
cognitive distortions, and family factors such as harsh discipline practices, low levels of parental warmth and acceptance, multiple 
changes in family composition, and parental psychopathology. EACP addresses these risk factors with transdiagnostic intervention 
activities, including personal goal setting, emotional awareness and regulation, perspective-taking, social problem solving, and 
rehearsal opportunities.
In addition, there was a significant intervention impact for girls on their self-reported Personal Adjustment. Adolescents’ self- 
ratings of the quality of their personal relationships, and their levels of self-esteem and self-reliance comprised the BASC composite 
for personal adjustment. Female adolescents assigned to EACP appeared to have reported growth in their personal adjustment likely 
related to the self-regulatory, social problem solving, and decision-making EACP activities. This finding appears consistent with 
Coping Power effects in the elementary school years, as children receiving Coping Power, in comparison to control conditions, have 
been found to have higher levels of perceived social competence, and an improved internal locus of control, and expectations that 
aggressive solutions to interpersonal problems will not lead to positive social outcomes ( Lochman & Wells, 2002a, 2002b ). 
Intervention-induced improvements in youth personal adjustment may assist the youth to sustain their observed behavior changes and 
affect their long-term behavioral and emotional outcomes.
It is noteworthy that given the older age group enrolled in the EACP, we were able to examine student-reported outcomes, whereas 
other studies of Coping Power to date have predominately focused on teacher and caregiver report. Although teachers observed and 
reported significant intervention effects for EACP on externalizing symptoms, in general, students’ reports of their externalizing 
behavior were not affected by their assignment to EACP or to the control condition. Similarly, we observed an effect of EACP on 
teacher-reported Internalizing Problems, in the context of higher baseline levels of these challenges, but student reports showed that 
those in the EACP intervention had increased self-reported Internalizing Problems. Such discrepancies in ratings are not uncommon in 
the research ( De Los Reyes et al., 2023 ). For example, Chen et al. (2009) examined externalizing behavior scores from teachers, 
adolescent students and peers on Frick’s Clustering Child Behavior Checklist and when the three sources were compared, the lowest 
correlations were between teacher and student, and the highest were between teachers and peers (so both were behavior observers). 
Taken together, these studies raise questions about the validity of student report symptoms over teacher ratings, leading us to put more 
weight on the teacher ratings than youth self-report, as the teachers may have a better sense of the students’ behavior than the stu -
dents’ self-ratings, particularly the externalizing symptoms. Additional research is needed to better understand the sources of dis -
crepancies by respondents ( De Los Reyes et al., 2023 ).
7.1. Limitations and future directions
We report findings from a large trial with 709 students across 40 schools, with randomization conducted at the school-level. Despite 
the randomized design, baseline equivalence tests suggested some imbalance at baseline; specifically, there was baseline inequivalence 
on two of the teacher-reported BASC subscales, but none of the other ten scales. Baseline inequivalence is a common source of bias in 
C.P. Bradshaw et al.                                                                                                                                                                                                  Journal of School Psychology 110 (2025) 101437 
11

trials of preventive interventions ( Steeger et al., 2021 ) and may be particularly problematic in group randomized trials; however, as 
noted in the results section, many of the intercept differences in those models fell to statistical non-significance. However, in the 
multilevel ITT models, the baseline differences between EACP and school as usual on teacher-reported Internalizing Problems persisted 
and there was a significant baseline difference denoted for student-reported Internalizing Problems. Sensitivity analyses including 
IPTW suggested a similar pattern of results, yet the main effect of EACP on teacher-reported Externalizing Problems fell below the p < 
.05 cutoff, while the point estimate was similar to the primary ITT effect ( γ
EACP 
= � 1.35, p = .076, d
GMA-RAW 
= � 0.21). Additionally, 
the EACP impact on teacher-reported Internalizing Problems in the IPTW models was statistically significant ( γ
EACP 
= � 1.92, p = .032, 
d
GMA-RAW 
= � 0.37). However, this impact is likely best characterized in the context of the baseline by intervention interaction.
We did not adjust for multiple comparisons in the final outcome analyses. We noted that emerging theoretical and empirical ev -
idence by Clifford et al. (2020) suggests that Coping Power may have transdiagnostic impacts on internalizing problems. As such, we 
organized our primary hypotheses to focus on teacher-reported externalizing problems, and our secondary outcomes related to 
teacher- and student-reported symptoms, with this latter set of outcomes being largely exploratory, as were the internalizing outcomes 
and the effect modification analyses. Therefore, we did not apply more conservative corrections like Bonferroni for the multiple tests 
(see Schochet, 2009 for a framework for applying corrections for multiple comparisons in impact evaluations).
Differential attrition was observed by intervention condition and other covariates. Differential attrition is a common concern in 
RCTs of preventive interventions that can lead to bias ( Steeger et al., 2021 ). We utilized FIML to account for missing data and included 
all covariates associated with attrition (including intervention condition), which should produce unbiased estimates of the inter -
vention effects under the MAR assumption ( Enders & Bandalos, 2001 ; Steeger et al., 2021 ). Given the robust array of covariates 
included at the student and school level across all models, we believe that the MAR assumption is tenable.
The first gate of the screening process for this study involved teacher ratings of students ’ aggressive behaviors. Although teacher 
ratings of children ’ s behavior problems have been found to be valid in predicting students ’ long-term outcomes, and to be superior to 
parent ratings ( Hill et al., 2004 ; Kassing et al., 2019 ; Whipp et al., 2019 ), the potential for teacher bias in behavior ratings is a lim -
itation. Teacher race has been found to affect their ratings of children ’ s behavior from the preschool years ( Wymer et al., 2022 ) 
through the later school years ( Redding, 2019 ), with White teachers rating Black children as having higher rates of externalizing 
behavior problems than do Black teachers. There is also evidence that teachers ’ misperceptions of Black children ’ s behavior can be 
influenced by implicit biases, and teachers ’ racially-biased perceptions of children ’ s disruptive behavior can lead to Black children 
receiving disproportionate discipline ( Girvan et al., 2017 ). We did not collect demographic data on teachers, as they were not enrolled 
as participants in the project; rather, they were considered raters of student outcomes and, in some cases, helped to deliver EACP 
program content. As a result, we lack data to examine teacher biases in screening. Moreover, there is little research examining whether 
and how such data should be used to adjust screening data for eligibility to participate in similar interventions. More research is needed 
to inform the approach to be taken adjusting for teacher-student racial mismatch and potential teacher racial bias in future studies 
using a similar screening approach.
Strength of the study include the large sample and random assignment across schools to deal with the potential of contamination; 
while it is possible that 6th grade teachers conducting the screening or the 8th grade teachers conducting the follow-up ratings may 
have become aware of the schools intervention condition over time, this seems unlikely given the relatively small number of students 
participating in the intervention and the fact they had no other interaction with the program components. It is possible that the 7th 
grade teachers who conducted the baseline and end of Year 1 BASC ratings were aware of the schools ’ intervention status over time, 
which could have influenced their ratings; some of these teachers were also “ cheer teachers ” in the intervention schools.
The findings presented here focused on the ITT analyses, and results are based on project hired implementers, at one-year follow- 
up. Future analyses will explore additional subgroup effects, as well as variation based on additional baseline characteristics (e.g., 
academic challenges) and possible mediators (e.g., social-cognitive changes). We also intend to explore other possible mechanisms of 
the change process, like the specific social cognitive factors consistent with the theory of change process as originally theorized and 
outlined by Lochman and Wells (2003) . Examination of other outcomes like academic performance and discipline problems, including 
suspensions and attendance, will be conducted once data are obtained from the participating school divisions. The overall fidelity of 
implementation was high for students ( Pas et al., 2020 ), although there was more variability in caregiver attendance, which could 
prove to be an important effect modifier to explore in future studies, as it has been shown to be in prior Coping Power studies ( Lindsey 
et al., 2019 ); this is a factor to be explored in relation to student outcomes in future studies.
8. Conclusion
Our findings suggest that EACP is a promising model for addressing early adolescents ’ mental and behavioral health needs in 
schools. Of particular interest are the potential crossover effects on internalizing symptoms, which highlight the transdiagnostic utility 
of EACP. Given the increasing need for programs to help early adolescents bridge the middle school transition, especially in light of 
elevated challenges during the COVID-19 pandemic recovery and the escalating youth mental health crisis ( OSG, 2021 ; Weist et al., 
2024 ), EACP holds promise for helping school psychologists meet some of the growing needs for evidence-based indicated in -
terventions in middle schools and overcoming barriers to services.

A systematic scoping review of peer support
interventions in integrated primary youth mental
health care

Eilis Hennessy 1 | Ailbhe Booth 1,2

Dublin (UCD), Dublin, Ireland

Jigsaw— The National Centre for Youth

Rachel Murphy, School of Psychology,
University College Dublin, Dublin, Ireland.
Email: rachel.murphy12@ucdconnect.ie
Funding information
Jigsaw–The National Centre for Youth
Mental Health; Irish Research Council
Abstract
Peer support, defined as the social and emotional support
offered and received by individuals with a shared experi-
ence of mental health difficulties, is gaining popularity in
youth mental health settings. This systematic scoping
review aimed to collate and synthesise the evidence on
key aspects of peer support interventions within integrated
youth services and educational settings. Specifically, it
synthesised evidence on the (1) assessed mental health
outcomes in peer support interventions, (2) key character-
istics and associated roles of peer support workers (PSWs)
and (3) barriers and facilitators to implementation. A search
of peer reviewed articles from January 2005 to June 2022
across five electronic databases (PsychINFO, Pubmed,
Scopus, ERIC and CINAHL) was conducted. A total of 15
studies retrieved in the search met the inclusion criteria and
were included in the review. This review supports previous
research indicating that peer support has potential for
improving recovery related outcomes. While a variety of
interventions and PSW roles were reported, studies could
be strengthened by providing more in ‐depth information
on intervention content. Examples of barriers to implemen-
tation included staff concerns around confidentiality of

1 | BACKGROUND
The term youth encompasses the developmental phases of adolescence and emerging adulthood, and spans the
early teenage years to the mid ‐twenties (Sawyer et al., 2018). During this period, young people experience
significant biological, psychological, and social changes (Birchwood & Singh, 2013) which can place them at
potential risk of psychological vulnerability. Mental health difficulties are prevalent, with anxiety and depression
among the top five causes of ill health in ages 15 –29 (The Lancet, 2022). Conversely, young people often do not or
cannot access adequate mental health support (McMahon et al., 2019). Several micro and macro level barriers can
prevent help seeking, including financial and structural barriers, or perceived stigma around mental health (Radez
et al., 2022; Salaheddin & Mason, 2016).
To address these barriers and increase access to care, an international movement has recently emerged with the aim
of providing youth specific services (McGorry et al.,2022; McMahon et al., 2019; O'Reilly et al., 2022). Integrated primary
youth mental health care models have been designed to acknowledge the complex psychological and social challenges
young people experience (McGorry et al., 2022). Two prominent settings in which this model has been adopted are
specialist integrated youth services (IYSs) and school and university mental health services (Duffy et al., 2019;H e t r i c k
et al., 2017; Kutcher & Wei, 2020; McGorry et al., 2022). Although the two settings differ in their structure, both are
recognised as important locations in which young people can access localised, community based, early intervention mental
health support (Duffy et al., 2019;K u t c h e r&W e i ,2020; McGorry et al., 2022).
IYSs are identified by several characteristics. Most notably, they are accessible in terms of location and cost,
they are located in highly visible and community spaces, they offer non ‐judgemental and non ‐stigmatising support,
and they involve young people in service design and delivery (Hetrick et al., 2017; McGorry et al., 2022; Settipani
et al., 2019). Notable examples of these services include Jigsaw, Ireland, formally Headstrong (established 2006),
ACCESS Open Minds, Canada (established 2014) and Maisons des Adolescents, France (established 1999).
Similarly, educational settings have been recognised as ideal locations to address students' mental health needs
given that they constitute single settings in which a large proportion of young people can be reached (Kutcher &
Wei, 2020; McMahon et al., 2019). Although not as rigorously defined as IYSs, school and university services are
beginning to adopt this model by implementing a range of accessible, youth friendly and evidence ‐based mental
health interventions within their community (Duffy et al., 2019; Kutcher & Wei, 2020). Examples of this approach
can be seen in tertiary education in the United Kingdom (Newton et al., 2016) and Ireland (Armstrong ‐Astley
et al., 2022; Hill et al., 2020), as well as secondary education in Canada (Kutcher & Wei, 2020) and Australia
(Anderson & Cooke, & Zbukvic, 2020).
To ensure integrated primary youth mental health (YMH) care models are developmentally appropriate and
person centred, it is recommended that young people are at the heart of service delivery (Halsall et al., 2021;
O'Reilly et al., 2022; Rickwood et al., 2019; Zbukvic et al., 2020). One component of youth participation that is
gaining attention is the involvement of young people through peer support (Anderson & Cooke, & Zbukvic, 2020;
Hennessy et al., 2022; Osborn et al., 2022).

Peer support, in the context of mental health, is defined as the social and emotional support offered and
received by people who have a lived experience of a mental health difficulty, with the aim of bringing about social
or personal change (Davidson et al., 2005). Solomon ( 2004) outlines six categories of peer support interventions: (1)
peer support groups, (2) internet support groups, (3) peer ‐delivered services, (4) peer ‐operated services (planned
and delivered by peer support workers [PSW]), (5) peer partnerships (a sponsoring organisation shares
administration and governance, but primary control is with peers), and (6) peer employees (individuals who
identify as having a mental illness who are hired into unique peer positions or who are employed to serve traditional
mental health positions).
Interest in peer support interventions is growing within IYSs and educational settings for several reasons
(Halsall et al., 2021; Mantzios, 2019). Firstly, peer relationships become increasingly important during youth which
is a peak developmental period for the emergence of mental health difficulties (Simmons et al., 2023). Peers are a
natural resource in the help seeking process, with young people citing friends as the most common source of mental
health information and support (Dooley et al., 2019). Additionally, the principles of peer support differ from
traditional client‐clinician relationships in that they are purposeful, where both young people (or groups of people)
form a mutual connection, share knowledge and viewpoints, and support and challenge one another to move
forward (Mead, 2005; Repper & Carter, 2011). As a result, peer support may be an entry way to mental health care
for those hesitant to seek help from a professional (Simmons et al., 2023).
Emerging evidence suggests that this interest is warranted. Recent reviews have indicated that researchers are
beginning to recognise peer support as a whole of life, rather than illness approach to mental health care
(Gillard, 2019) and are measuring mental health outcomes accordingly. Several reviews of the adult peer support
literature have identified improvements in quality of life, empowerment, hope and recovery (Bellamy et al., 2017;
Chinman et al., 2014; Lloyd ‐Evans et al., 2014; White et al., 2020). Similar findings have been noted in reviews of
formal and informal peer support in a variety of YMH and community settings such as improved psychological
wellbeing, empowerment, self ‐esteem, and social functioning (Gopalan et al., 2017; Richard et al., 2022). In
educational settings, evidence suggests that peer support can increase self ‐confidence, self ‐esteem, self ‐
management, hope, empowerment and reduce loneliness (Johnson et al., 2018; King & Fazel, 2021; White
et al., 2020).
Overall, the available evidence on peer support for YMH is promising. However, much of this comes from
reviews of peer support in community settings or secondary/tertiary mental health services (Gopalan et al., 2017;
Richard et al., 2022; Simmons et al., 2023). A comprehensive overview of the reported changes in mental health
outcomes is essential to confirm these benefits in integrated YMH care, and explore further potential benefits
(Anderson & Zbukvic, 2020).
In addition to the understanding the impact of peer support on mental health, a key priority for research is
identifying the features of PSWs involved in these interventions (King & Simmons, 2018). PSWs are predominately
defined as people with experiences of mental health difficulties. In YMH settings, similarity in age is also often
understood to be a requirement for the role (de Beer et al., 2022; Fava et al., 2020). Both lived experience and age
can enhance the relationship between a young person and PSW by increasing the likelihood of shared experiences
(Fava et al., 2020). However, although these characteristics are recommended, it is unclear whether services are
recruiting PSWs based on these requirements.
It is also recommended that peer support is introduced to services systematically, such that PSWs are recruited
with the above ‐mentioned characteristics in mind and are provided with opportunities for professional
development. Adequate training and appropriate supervision are particularly important in youth settings, given
the age and lack of employment experience of young people recruited into these roles (de Beer et al., 2022).
Furthermore, role requirements should be made clear to PSWs and staff working within a service (Collins
et al., 2016). Lack of clarity can result in PSWs experiencing difficulties integrating with services (Collins
et al., 2016); feeling tokenistic (Kilpatrick et al., 2017); or conversely, becoming overworked or burnt ‐out in their
role (de Beer et al., 2022). de Beer et al. ( 2022) previously investigated roles of PSWs across youth settings,

including psychiatric care, community mental health services and youth offending services. They found that PSWs
performed a variety of roles, including advocating for peers, planning treatment, educating peers and providing
emotional support. In primary and community mental health care, Fava et al. ( 2020) suggests that PSWs provide
face‐to‐face and/or group peer work, predominately focusing on shared experiences, recovery goals, and a broad
focus on mental health. However, they also note that these roles can vary significantly from service to service. As
such, it is unclear if there are consistent roles across integrated primary YMH care. Thus, while it is evident that
there are potentially unique characteristics and role requirements for PSWs in these settings, no review to date has
synthesised these data, which would be of value to services in the development of role requirements and training
programmes.
Finally, it should be noted that successful peer support interventions do not solely rely on the relationship
between a young person and PSW, but also on the organisation and the staff working within it (Hopkins
et al., 2021). Previous reviews of peer support in adult mental health services suggest that several barriers can
impede implementation of these interventions. These include staff concerns or confusion around the role of PSWs;
inadequate recruitment processes, and lack of training and supervision (Hopkins et al., 2021; Ibrahim et al., 2020).
Conversely, facilitators of peer support in adult settings include strong leadership, organisational readiness, rigorous
recruitment strategies, and team integration (Kent, 2019; Mutschler et al., 2022; Zeng & McNamara, 2021).
Although systematic reviews have examined implementation of adult peer support (Ibrahim et al., 2020; Mutschler
et al., 2022; Zeng & McNamara, 2021), no review to date has examined the barriers and facilitators to peer support
in YMH settings, despite this being a recognised priority for future research (Anderson & Zbukvic, 2020).
Furthermore, Chinman et al. ( 2017) emphasise the need to examine the current literature on barriers and facilitators
at the organisational level through implementation frameworks such as The Consolidated Framework for
Implementation Research (CFIR). Such frameworks are valuable in understanding how and why a specific
intervention is or is not implemented successfully (Damschroder et al., 2009).
1.1 | The present study
While interest in peer support for YMH care is growing, a substantial gap remains in our understanding of these
interventions in the context of integrated primary YMH care, including IYSs and educational settings. The aim of this
scoping review is to identify, collate and synthesise the available evidence on key aspects of peer support
interventions within IYSs, and school and university settings. Specifically, this review aims to address the following
research questions:
(1) What mental health outcomes have been reported in studies of peer support interventions in integrated
primary YMH care?
(2) What are the core characteristics of PSWs and their subsequent roles in integrated primary YMH care?
(3) What are the barriers and facilitators to implementing peer support interventions in integrated primary youth
care mental health care?
2 | METHODS
A systematic scoping review methodology was employed to answer the research questions. Scoping reviews are a
type of evidence synthesis that aim to systematically map the available research in a given area and to identify
gaps in the field of research (Arksey & O'Malley, 2005; Munn et al., 2018, 2022). A research protocol
was developed in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta ‐Analyses
(PRISMA‐ScR) extension for Scoping Reviews (Tricco et al., 2018) and registered on Open Science Framework;

https://osf.io/fbq2n/. The review was carried out in distinct phases, as described by Arksey and O'Malley ( 2005),
including the development of the research questions; identification of potentially relevant studies; screening and
selecting papers; charting the data from included papers; collating, summarising, and reporting the results; and
consultation with stakeholders.
2.1 | Youth participation
A participatory approach was adopted to develop the research questions. During initial planning stages, a
consultation was held with the Youth Research Council (YRC) at (YMH service). The YRC was comprised of a group
of young people (aged 20 –25; n = 8) with lived experiences of mental health difficulties and/or an interest in YMH.
The lead author met the group via Zoom for a 1 ‐h meeting and discussed the purpose of the review and initial
research questions. The collaborative platform, Padlet was used to collate feedback from members. The YRC
confirmed that the research questions were appropriate for capturing the available evidence on peer support in
integrated primary YMH care.
2.2 | Search strategy
A search strategy was developed through the identification of terms used in reviews of a similar nature (e.g.,
Gopalan et al., 2017; Richard et al., 2022) and consultation with a specialist librarian. The strategy was informed by
the PCC (Population of Interest, Concept, Context) model for scoping reviews (Peters et al., 2020). Terms related to
youth (population), peer support (concept) and YMH services/schools and universities (context) were included in
the search. Following preliminary assessment of electronic databases for their relevance and coverage of the topic
literature, five databases were identified to carry out the electronic search: Psychinfo, Pubmed, ERIC, CINAHL and
Scopus. These databases were searched for peer ‐reviewed articles between September 1st 2005 and June 2022
13th 2022. The full search string is presented in Supporting Information Material . To avoid missing relevant
publications, backwards and forwards citation searching was conducted on studies selected for data extraction.
2.3 | Inclusion and exclusion criteria
Studies were included if they met the following criteria:
(a) The studied intervention was aimed at young people between 12 and 25 years, or the majority of participants
fell within this range.
(b) The studied intervention was aimed at young people experiencing mild to moderate mental health difficulties.
(c) The study involved a PSW(s) partially or fully delivering the intervention.
(d) The studied intervention was delivered within an IYS or a secondary or tertiary educational setting.
(e) The study was published in a peer reviewed publication.
(f) The study was published in English, between 1st September 2005 and June 13th 2022.
For the purpose of this review, mild to moderate mental difficulties were defined as any mental, behavioural or
emotional condition which do not require specialist intervention (e.g., inpatient care) and that are typically managed
in primary care or go undiagnosed. The review excluded peer support interventions aimed at young people with
serious mental illness, such as bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, psychotic conditions or any disorder resulting in
serious functional impairment (National Institute of Youth Mental Health, 2023). Following an initial search of the

literature, a decision was made to limit the search from 2005. This decision was made for several reasons. Firstly,
the vast majority of IYSs launched following the conception of the headspace model in 2006 (McGorry et al., 2022).
Secondly, evidence suggests that schools and universities began to take a more integrated approach to mental
health care within the last decade (Duffy et al., 2019). Thus, an increased focus on peer support in these settings is
likely to have occurred post 2005.
In this review, studies were excluded if they: were literature, scoping or systematic reviews, study protocols,
book chapters, dissertations, conference abstracts or editorials; examined informal peer support, for example peer
support provided by friends; examined peer support for non ‐mental health outcomes, for example academic, social
or occupational outcomes; did not include a PSW or were fully delivered by a mental health practitioner.
2.4 | Study selection
Search results from each database were exported to the reference management software Zotero ( https://www.
zotero.org/) and then exported to Covidence Systematic Review Software ( https://www.covidence.org/) for title,
abstract and full text screening. Title and abstracts of studies were screened independently by two reviewers (XX
and XX). Studies selected for full text review were screened again by the two independent reviewers (XX and XX).
As recommended by Levac et al. ( 2010) reviewers met regularly throughout this screening process to resolve any
uncertainties. A third reviewer was consulted (XX), where a decision regarding inclusion could not be reached.
Following full text review, citation searching and manual searching of previous reviews was completed by XX, and
any potentially relevant articles were added to Covidence, and the process was followed through again.
2.5 | Data extraction and analysis
After study selection, the first reviewer (XX) conducted data extraction on all included articles. A second reviewer
(XX) conducted extraction on 50% of studies to ensure consistency. The extraction tool was developed through an
iterative process which consisted of consultation with the research team (XX & XX) and the completion of pilot
extractions. Key information that was extracted included: (a) study characteristics (authors, title, date of publication,
journal, country of origin, study aim and study design), (b) characteristics of the intervention (setting, type of peer
support, purpose of peer support intervention), (c) participant characteristics (age, gender, presenting mental health
problems), (d) mental health outcomes (outcome measures and reported changes) and (e) peer worker
characteristics (role, characteristics, training and supervision).
To address the question on implementation, qualitative studies were coded using the domains of the CFIR
(https://cfirguide.org/; Damschroder et al., 2009). The CFIR is a meta ‐theoretical framework consisting of five
domains that influence whether or not an intervention is successfully implemented: Intervention Characteristics ,
Outer Setting (features of the external context or environment that might influence implementation e.g. external
policies and incentives), Inner Setting (features of the implementing organisations that might influence
implementation e.g. organisation culture, leadership engagement, available resources), Characteristics of Individuals
and Implementation Processes . The CFIR has been used widely in implementation science studies across a range of
health service research contexts and has previously been used in reviews examining implementation of peer
support interventions in adult settings (Mutschler et al., 2022).
Using this framework, the first reviewer (XX) read the a rticles discussing imple mentation and highlighted
the phrases and sections that mentioned this process. The highlighted sections were then grouped together
by common themes and organised into the appropriat e construct within the CFIR framework. The second
reviewer (XX) also reviewed the final themes to ensu re that themes correspond ed with the appropriate
domains (Figure 1).

3 | RESULTS
3.1 | General overview
A total of fifteen studies were included for analysis in the final review (Table 1). All of the studies were published
between 2011 and 2022, with ten studies (66%) published after 2020. All included studies were conducted in high
income countries; five in Australia (1, 6, 10, 11, 12), four in the United States (2, 5, 8, 14), three in Canada (4, 7, 15),
two in the United Kingdom (3, 13) and one in the Netherlands (9). The study methodology ranged across studies,
five were randomised control trials (2, 5, 6, 7, 14), one was a non ‐randomised trial (10), two were cohort studies

3.2 | Characteristics of peer support interventions in integrated primary YMH care
Thirteen peer support interventions were included in the review; three studies were conducted on the same
intervention; The Choice Project (10, 11, 12). The most common setting for peer support interventions was within
university services (2, 3, 5, 6, 7, 14, 15), followed by integrated YMH services (4, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12). One intervention
was delivered through an online YMH service (1), and one was delivered in a secondary/high school in collaboration
with a community mental health service (13).
A variety of interventions based on Soloman's (2004) categorisation were reported. Four studies were self ‐help
groups (3, 4, 5, 14). Four studies involved peer delivered one ‐to‐one support (2, 7, 8, 13). Three studies involved
peer employees providing one ‐to‐one support (10, 11, 12). Two studies examined peer operated services, also using
one‐to‐one support (9, 15). The two remaining studies examined internet support groups (1, 6).
Nine out of the thirteen interventions were aimed at young people aged 16 –25 years (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 10, 11,
12, 14, 15). One intervention was aimed at adolescents aged 13 –19 years (8), one was delivered to adolescents
aged 11–18 years (13) and one intervention was aimed at young people aged 12 –25 years, although the age range
of attendees ranged from 10 to 55 years (9).
Eight of the thirteen interventions were targeted at young people experiencing any mild to moderate mental
health difficulty such as depression, anxiety, and psychological distress (1, 3, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 15). One
intervention was aimed at young people who had concerns about their mental health and experienced self ‐stigma
around disclosing their mental health difficulties to others (5). Two interventions targeted young people at risk of
developing a mental health difficulty; attendees (secondary school students) of one interventions (13) were self ‐
referred or referred by teachers if considered to be at risk of developing a mental health problem. Similarly, another
programme (14) targeted female college students experiencing body dissatisfaction. One intervention was aimed at
young people with social anxiety (2).
